<user_action>
  <context>User initiated a review task. Here's the full review output from reviewer model. User may select one or more comments to resolve.</context>
  <action>review</action>
  <results>
  The application code changes look sound, but the untracked `.codex/config.toml` introduces a hard-coded local binary path that will break the notify hook anywhere except the author's machine if it is included in the patch.

Review comment:

- [P2] Replace the notify command's machine-specific binary path — /home/teslamint/Workspace/entirecontext/.codex/config.toml:2-2
  If this repo-local config is committed as-is, `codex-notify` will only work on the author's machine because the first command element is an absolute path under `/home/teslamint`. On any other workstation or CI runner with a different home directory or install prefix, hook execution will fail with a missing-file error. Please keep this file untracked or switch it to a portable command resolved via `PATH`.
  </results>
  </user_action>
assistant_summary:
The application code changes look sound, but the untracked `.codex/config.toml` introduces a hard-coded local binary path that will break the notify hook anywhere except the author's machine if it is included in the patch.

Review comment:

- [P2] Replace the notify command's machine-specific binary path — /home/teslamint/Workspace/entirecontext/.codex/config.toml:2-2
  If this repo-local config is committed as-is, `codex-notify` will only work on the author's machine because the first command element is an absolute path under `/home/teslamint`. On any other workstation or CI runner with a different home directory or install prefix, hook execution will fail with a missing-file error. Please keep this file untracked or switch it to a portable command resolved via `PATH`.
I’m checking the repo-local config and surrounding hook setup first so the fix plan is grounded in the actual current state.
I’m implementing the ignore-based fix now. First I’m checking whether any tracked docs mention `.codex/config.toml` so I can keep behavior and guidance aligned with the review comment.
I’m committing only the `.gitignore` change for the P2 fix and leaving the unrelated untracked `error.log` alone.

user_message:
<user_action>
  <context>User initiated a review task. Here's the full review output from reviewer model. User may select one or more comments to resolve.</context>
  <action>review</action>
  <results>
  The new checkpoint import ordering fixes one FK issue, but it also introduces a hard failure on deep parent chains and a false-positive parent existence check when ids share prefixes. Either case can cause `perform_pull` to abort on valid-looking data.

Full review comments:

- [P1] Replace recursive parent ordering before long checkpoint chains overflow — /home/teslamint/Workspace/entirecontext/src/entirecontext/sync/coordinator.py:271-278
  When a pull brings in a long linear checkpoint history, `_visit()` recurses once per ancestor. With this project’s checkpoint-per-commit flow, importing roughly 1000+ new checkpoints in one lineage will hit Python’s recursion limit and abort the entire pull with `RecursionError`. The previous implementation was iterative, so this failure mode is introduced by the new DFS and should be rewritten with an explicit stack or other iterative ordering.

- [P2] Use exact checkpoint existence when deciding to clear missing parents — /home/teslamint/Workspace/entirecontext/src/entirecontext/sync/coordinator.py:298-300
  The new missing-parent fallback calls `get_checkpoint(conn, parent_id)`, but that helper intentionally does prefix matching. If the DB contains `cp-parent-v2` and the imported child references missing `cp-parent`, this check returns a false positive, `parent_id` is kept, and `create_checkpoint()` still fails the foreign-key insert because no checkpoint with the exact id exists. This only shows up when ids share prefixes, but the codebase already uses short human-readable ids in tests and CLI paths.
  </results>
  </user_action>
assistant_summary:
The new checkpoint import ordering fixes one FK issue, but it also introduces a hard failure on deep parent chains and a false-positive parent existence check when ids share prefixes. Either case can cause `perform_pull` to abort on valid-looking data.

Full review comments:

- [P1] Replace recursive parent ordering before long checkpoint chains overflow — /home/teslamint/Workspace/entirecontext/src/entirecontext/sync/coordinator.py:271-278
  When a pull brings in a long linear checkpoint history, `_visit()` recurses once per ancestor. With this project’s checkpoint-per-commit flow, importing roughly 1000+ new checkpoints in one lineage will hit Python’s recursion limit and abort the entire pull with `RecursionError`. The previous implementation was iterative, so this failure mode is introduced by the new DFS and should be rewritten with an explicit stack or other iterative ordering.

- [P2] Use exact checkpoint existence when deciding to clear missing parents — /home/teslamint/Workspace/entirecontext/src/entirecontext/sync/coordinator.py:298-300
  The new missing-parent fallback calls `get_checkpoint(conn, parent_id)`, but that helper intentionally does prefix matching. If the DB contains `cp-parent-v2` and the imported child references missing `cp-parent`, this check returns a false positive, `parent_id` is kept, and `create_checkpoint()` still fails the foreign-key insert because no checkpoint with the exact id exists. This only shows up when ids share prefixes, but the codebase already uses short human-readable ids in tests and CLI paths.
I’m inspecting the checkpoint import path and current tests first so the fix plan covers the actual failure modes the review found.
I’ve got the current sync diff loaded. I’m updating the pull path in place so it stays compatible with the existing FK-ordering fix while removing the recursion and prefix-match failure modes.
I’m committing the sync fix and tests only, then I’ll push that commit. I’m checking the worktree first so I don’t include unrelated files.
